did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a scaled 2D-CNN based on the EfficientNet-B7 deep neural network architecture, along with pre-trained models VGG19 and ResNet-50, to classify benign and malignant skin cancer tumors using dermoscopic images from the ISIC Archive dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a scaled 2D-CNN based on EfficientNet-B7 for skin cancer diagnosis. It details a specific methodology including image pre-processing, model training on the ISIC Archive dataset, and reports new quantitative results (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) comparing the proposed model against VGG19 and ResNet-50. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE CIBCB), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Scaled-2D CNN for Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: 2021 IEEE Conference on Computational Intelligence in Bioinformatics and Computational Biology (CIBCB)
brief_summary: This study proposes a scaled 2D-CNN based on the EfficientNet-B7 architecture to classify benign and malignant skin cancer tumors using dermoscopic images from the ISIC Archive dataset. The methodology includes image pre-processing techniques such as resizing, conversion, and augmentation before feeding the data into the model. The proposed architecture's performance was evaluated against pre-trained models VGG19 and ResNet-50 using metrics including AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score. The results demonstrated that the proposed EfficientNet-B7 model outperformed the comparative architectures, achieving an accuracy of 0.987, an AUC of 0.993, and an F1-score of 0.93. The authors conclude that the proposed model is highly effective for automated skin cancer diagnosis and suitable for early screening applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 1440 benign training images and 360 benign test images.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 1197 malignant training images and 300 malignant test images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer tumors into benign and malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is a 'two class classification problem' to distinguish between 'benign and malignant skin cancer'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC Archive dataset. Performance was evaluated using metrics typical for classification such as accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin cancer dermoscopic image dataset retrieved from the ISIC Archive (specifically referenced as ISIC 2017 in the references). It contains dermoscopic images of skin tumors.

use.training: Used for training the proposed scaled 2D-CNN (EfficientNet-B7), VGG19, and ResNet-50 models. Images were pre-processed via resize, conversion, and augmentation.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed system and comparing performance against other models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed scaled 2D-CNN, ResNet-50, and VGG-19 models for binary classification.
To measure the performance of the model during training by evaluating the difference between predicted probabilities and actual labels.
To optimize the model weights during training for the proposed EfficientNet-B7 architecture.
To optimize the model weights during training for the VGG-19 architecture.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed scaled 2D-CNN and pre-trained models (VGG19, ResNet-50) were based on descriptive metrics (AUC, accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive Dataset.description: Binary classification of benign vs malignant skin tumors. Dataset size: 3297 original images (2637 training, 660 testing). Pre-processed dataset size: 10,351 images (7248 training, 3106 testing). Split: 70% training, 30% testing.
ISIC Archive Dataset.notes: The proposed EfficientNet-B7 model outperformed VGG-19 and ResNet-50. The paper also compares against other existing frameworks in Table II: Waheed et al. (SVM, 0.96 accuracy on PH2), Shihadeh et al. (AlexNet, 0.74 accuracy on ISIC), Han et al. (ResNet-152, 0.96 accuracy on ISIC), Mahbod et al. (ResNet-18, 0.976 accuracy on ISIC), Albahar (Tuned-CNN, 0.975 accuracy on ISIC), and Shahin et al. (ResNet-50+Inception V3, 0.899 accuracy on ISIC).

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resize: Images were resized to 224x224 pixels to ensure uniform input dimensions for the deep convolutional neural network.

Image Conversion: Grayscale images were converted to 3-channel RGB images using an average method to match the input requirements of the model; some images were also converted from RGB to grayscale for edge recognition purposes.

Image Augmentation: Artificial data was generated from the training dataset using techniques including random rotation, shift, flips in different angles, shear, zoom, and noise invariance to improve classifier robustness.

conclusions: The proposed scaled 2D-CNN based on the EfficientNet-B7 architecture outperformed pre-trained models VGG19 and ResNet-50 in the binary classification of benign and malignant skin cancer tumors.
The proposed model achieved high performance metrics on the ISIC Archive dataset, specifically an AUC of 0.993, accuracy of 0.987, specificity of 0.97, sensitivity of 0.98, and F1-score of 0.93.
The study demonstrates that the proposed EfficientNet-B7 based model is highly effective for automated skin cancer diagnosis and suitable for early screening applications.
The authors conclude that the proposed model can be deployed for industrial purposes to assist doctors and general users in skin cancer detection.

The study acknowledges that prior image pre-processing (including resize, conversion, and augmentation) is necessary for the proposed model to achieve high accuracy; without it, performance would be significantly lower.
The proposed model is considered capable of being used for future medical diagnoses on other large datasets beyond the ISIC Archive.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) and does not distinguish between specific malignant subtypes such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, or squamous cell carcinoma, despite mentioning them in the introduction.
The dataset used (ISIC Archive) is relatively small (3,297 original images), which may limit the generalizability of the results compared to larger, multi-center clinical datasets.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the performance differences between the proposed model and the comparative models (VGG19, ResNet-50).

There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets; the model was only evaluated on the same ISIC Archive dataset used for training and testing, raising potential concerns about overfitting or dataset bias.

The performance relies heavily on extensive image pre-processing (resize, conversion, augmentation); the authors note that without this pre-processing, the model cannot achieve high accuracy, which may limit its robustness in real-world scenarios with unprocessed images.
The paper claims suitability for 'industrial purposes' and 'daily applications' but provides no evidence of clinical deployment, user studies, or integration into clinical workflows.
The comparison with other works in the related works section uses heterogeneous datasets (e.g., PH2, ISIC) and metrics, making direct performance comparisons difficult and potentially biased.
The test set size (660 images) is small, which may result in high variance in the reported metrics (AUC, accuracy, etc.) and reduce the reliability of the performance estimates.
